content.full_text:
August 11, 1976


MR. Richard Cotton
Natural Resources Defense
Council, Inc.
2345 Yale Street
Palo Alto, CA 94306

Dear Mr. Cotton:

My apologies for the delay in replying to your letter of
June 28, 1976. As I indicated earlier, the questions you posed in
that letter have been under consideration in recent weeks, and
consequently I was unable to provide advice prior to this time. In
response to your first question, you are not a lobbyist by virtue
of your service on the advisory committees to the Energy Commission.

Whether or not you are an "agency official," Government
Code Section 82004, because of serving on the boards depends on
whether the boards are considered to be "decision-making boards" or
purely advisory boards. I am enclosing a copy of a Commission
opinion concerning the Citizen's Advisory Committee of the Bay
Conservation and Development Commission. Opinion requested by
John C. Morrissey, 2 PRC Opinions (No. 75-039, July 6, 1976). The
Commission concluded that the BCDC Committee is purely advisory,
and therefore board membership entails no reporting obligations for
the persons who serve on the board or for their employers. That
opinion sets out the characteristics for determining whether a
board or committee has "decision-making authority," and should
apply the standards set forth in that opinion to your situation.

If you determine that the boards are advisory, you are
not an agency official and persons who discuss committee matters
with you are not subject to the lobbyist reporting obligations
because of those discussions.

I hope this information will be of assistance.

Sincerely,

/s/

Natalie B. West
Staff Counsel

NEW:plh
Enclosure

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

July 2, 1976

Mr. Richard Cotton
2345 Yale Street
Palo Alto, CA 94306

Dear Mr. Cotton:

Mr. Lowenstein has asked me to respond to your letter
of June 23, 1976, concerning your participation on advisory
committees to the State Energy Resources Conservation and
Development Commission. The questions which you raise are
currently under consideration by the Commission. The ap-
plication of the lobbyist provisions of the Political Reform
Act to advisory board membership is the subject of two opini-
ons that were considered by the Commission in June, and
will be considered again in early July. Rather than give
you interim advice that may be superseded within a few days,
I will respond to your letter in greater detail after that
meeting.

With regard to your lobbyist registration statement,
you should be aware that the Political Reform Act imposes
certain restrictions and reporting requirements on all persons who are registered as lobbyists and on their employers.
For example, as a registered lobbyist you will be prohibited
from making campaign contributions to elected state officers,
state candidates and committees supporting state candidates.
You will want to give careful consideration to such restrictions before becoming a registered lobbyist such regis-
tration is not legally required. In any event, if you decide
to register, the registration statement and required monthly
reports must be filed with the Secretary of State, not with
the Fair Political Practices Commission. I am returning
your registration statement so that you may take appropriate
action.

I hope this information will be of assistance.

Sincerely,

Natalie B. West
Staff Counsel

NEBslph
Enclosure

Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc.

664 HAMILTON AVENUE
PALO ALTO, CALIF. 94301

415 327-1080
2345 YALE STREET
PALO ALTO, CALIF. 94306

Washington Office
917 15TH STREET, N.W.
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20005
202 737-5000

New York Office
15 WEST 44TH STREET
NEW YORK, N.Y. 10036
212 869-0150

June 28, 1976

The Honorable Daniel Lowenstein
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street Building
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

I am writing to request guidance from the Commission on three matters related to my work before the State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission.

(1) I currently serve on two advisory committees to the Energy Commission. One advisory committee on Intermittent Ignition devices" was established pursuant to Public Resources Code Section 25961 which requires the Commission to "develop in cooperation with affected industry and consumer representatives, who will be designated as such representatives by the Commission, the specifications for certification of intermittent ignition devices." The second advisory committee on which I now serve is charged with the duty of developing building conservation standards for non-residential construction pursuant to Health and Safety Code sections 19878 et seq. Pursuant to Commission order, that Committee now includes a number of "special consultants" who serve in addition to the statutory members. I serve as one of the special consultants.

It is my understanding that the FPPC has tentatively decided that participation in such advisory committees does not create reporting obligations pursuant to the lobbyist provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act. I would like to request formally, however, the current views of the FPPC on this matter.

(2) I would like to inquire whether my participation on either of these committees brings me within the "public official" category of the Act, and therefore places lobbyist reporting obligations on others who may discuss with me the subjects covered in either of these committees.

100% Recycled Paper

The Honorable Daniel Lowenstein
June 28, 1976
Page two


     (3)  If the answer to my second question is affirmative to
any extent, I would like to know if "in-house" discussions with
other staff members at NRDC concerning subject matter relevant
to either committee could possibly subject those individuals to
reporting requirements under the Act.

     Finally, I enclose a completed lobbyist registration statement
for myself in keeping with NRDC's policy as enunciated in John
Leshy's letter to you in July of last year.  It appears that
under the current definition of lobbying under the Act, the
lobbyist reporting provisions of the Act do not cover my activities
before the Energy Commission which have been limited to participa-
tion in the advisory committees set out above.  However, to remove
any doubt that NRDC is complying with the Act's intent, I
enclose a lobbyist registration statement.  A lobbyist activity
authorization has previously been filed by NRDC.

     Based on my conclusion that my advisory committee activities
are not subject to the reporting requirements of the Act, I am
not submitting any lobbyist report forms.  I look forward to
receiving some guidance on the above matters.

                                        Sincerely yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Richard Cotton

RC:gg

Enclosure

sections.question: 1) Does participation in advisory committees to the Energy Commission create reporting obligations pursuant to the lobbyist provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act? 2) Does participation on these committees bring the person within the "public official" category, placing lobbyist reporting obligations on others who discuss committee subjects? 3) Could "in-house" discussions with other staff members concerning committee subject matter subject those individuals to reporting requirements?
sections.conclusion: Participation in purely advisory committees does not make one a lobbyist. Whether advisory committee service creates "agency official" status depends on whether the boards have decision-making authority. The Commission provides standards from Opinion No. 73-099 regarding the Citizen's Advisory Committee of the Bay Conservation and Development Commission to help determine if boards are advisory or decision-making. If the boards are purely advisory, there are no reporting obligations for board members or their employers, and persons discussing committee matters are not subject to lobbyist reporting.
sections.question_synthetic: A Natural Resources Defense Council staff member serving on advisory committees to the State Energy Commission seeks guidance on whether such service creates lobbyist reporting obligations for themselves or others who discuss committee matters with them.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that service on purely advisory committees does not trigger lobbyist status, and provides a test based on prior Commission opinions to determine whether committees are truly advisory versus decision-making bodies.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of three letters: incoming request dated June 23, 1975; interim response dated July 2, 1976; and final FPPC response dated August 11, 1976. The incoming request provides context; extraction focused on the August 11 FPPC response letter. Some OCR quality issues with document scans but core content clearly legible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) Does participation in advisory committees to the Energy Commission create reporting obligations pursuant to the lobbyist provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act? 2) Does participation on these committees bring the person within the "public official" category, placing lobbyist reporting obligations on others who discuss committee subjects? 3) Could "in-house" discussions with other staff members concerning committee subject matter subject those individuals to reporting requirements?

CONCLUSION: Participation in purely advisory committees does not make one a lobbyist. Whether advisory committee service creates "agency official" status depends on whether the boards have decision-making authority. The Commission provides standards from Opinion No. 73-099 regarding the Citizen's Advisory Committee of the Bay Conservation and Development Commission to help determine if boards are advisory or decision-making. If the boards are purely advisory, there are no reporting obligations for board members or their employers, and persons discussing committee matters are not subject to lobbyist reporting.

embedding.summary: This file contains correspondence between Richard Cotton of the Natural Resources Defense Council and the FPPC regarding whether his service on advisory committees to the State Energy Commission triggers lobbyist reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC ultimately advises that purely advisory committee membership does not create reporting obligations.
